name: Technical Demonstrator Design
consequences: Prioritizing FIDO2/WebAuthn-based authentication reduces reliance on platform-specific signals but may face adoption challenges due to existing ecosystem lock-in. A browser-based fallback path could expose gaps in current identity-provider infrastructure, necessitating upstream changes in public-sector system design.
options: Build a GrapheneOS-compatible FIDO2 authenticator for simulated public-sector identity providers
Develop a hardware-token-based fallback mechanism using open-standard cryptographic protocols
Create a browser-only authentication flow that bypasses mobile OS integrity checks
review: Technical feasibility vs. institutional adoption. Weakness: The options assume rapid public-sector infrastructure updates without addressing legacy system constraints.

name: Policy Framing Strategy
consequences: Reframing dependency risks as resilience failures could pressure Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to prioritize continuity planning, but may conflict with existing procurement norms favoring vendor-certified solutions. Metrics-based supplier concentration analysis could create urgency without clear mitigation pathways.
options: Position platform neutrality as a critical-resilience requirement in public-sector procurement guidelines
Quantify supplier concentration risks using anonymized app-distribution and authentication telemetry data
Link fallback authentication to democratic accountability through transparency in identity-provider decision-making
review: Resilience imperative vs. procurement tradition. Weakness: The options understate the political complexity of redefining 'security' in procurement criteria.

name: Coalition Engagement Model
consequences: Mobilizing privacy advocates and academic researchers could amplify technical credibility but risks alienating pragmatic stakeholders focused on service continuity. Cross-sector forums might create friction between idealistic and operational priorities.
options: Establish a multi-stakeholder working group with civil-society representatives and public-sector IT managers
Leverage academic research networks to validate fallback-authentication security claims
Form alliances with regional municipalities to pilot alternative authentication models
review: Ideological alignment vs. pragmatic collaboration. Weakness: The options lack mechanisms to engage service-providing agencies focused on user experience.

name: Procurement Influence Tactics
consequences: Conditioning funding on platform-neutral requirements could force AltID stakeholders to reconsider architecture, but may provoke resistance from vendors invested in current models. Benchmarking against EU standards might create regulatory pressure without immediate implementation mandates.
options: Embed fallback-authentication criteria in AltID procurement RFPs through conditional funding agreements
Develop a technical benchmarking framework for evaluating platform-neutral authentication solutions
Create a public-private partnership to co-fund pilot implementations of alternative authentication paths
review: Regulatory pressure vs. vendor resistance. Weakness: The options fail to account for the political power of incumbent platform providers.

name: Standards Alignment Approach
consequences: Advocating for EU-wide interoperability standards could create long-term momentum but may delay national implementation. Open-standard adoption risks fragmentation if not coordinated with eIDAS2 requirements.
options: Align fallback-authentication proposals with European Digital Identity Wallet technical specifications
Propose national standards for platform-neutral authentication that reference eIDAS2 compliance frameworks
Promote open-source implementation of fallback mechanisms to reduce vendor lock-in
review: European harmonization vs. national autonomy. Weakness: The options overlook the slow pace of EU regulatory adoption.

name: Alternative Authentication Ecosystems
consequences: Prioritizing non-platform authentication methods directly challenges Apple/Google's trust architecture but risks fragmentation without institutional backing. This could create interoperability gaps unless paired with procurement mandates.
options: Deploy hardware security keys with FIDO2 standards for high-assurance scenarios
Develop browser-based authentication using WebAuthn with decentralized identity providers
Establish a national open-source authentication library for platform-agnostic implementation
review: Technical viability vs. institutional adoption. Weakness: Options focus on implementation without addressing procurement enforcement mechanisms.

name: Public-Private Collaboration Frameworks
consequences: Forming industry coalitions could amplify advocacy power but may dilute technical rigor through compromise. Success depends on maintaining credibility with both regulators and developers.
options: Create a cross-sector consortium to jointly audit platform dependency risks
Initiate pilot programs with regional banks to test alternative authentication workflows
Develop shared technical benchmarks for platform-neutral identity verification
review: Collaboration efficiency vs. technical integrity. Weakness: Options lack mechanisms to prevent commercial interests from undermining public-sector goals.

name: Regulatory Safeguarding Mechanisms
consequences: Proposing mandatory fallback requirements could institutionalize platform neutrality but may face legal challenges from existing service providers. This creates a critical window for policy framing before technical lock-in occurs.
options: Draft legislation requiring public-sector systems to support open-standard authentication protocols
Implement mandatory third-party audits for platform dependency in critical infrastructure
Create a certification pathway for 'resilient identity' solutions compliant with eIDAS 2.0
review: Compliance enforcement vs. regulatory flexibility. Weakness: Options don't address potential legal conflicts with existing platform agreements.

name: Cross-Platform Interoperability Standards
consequences: Advocating open protocols could break vendor monopolies but requires overcoming entrenched technical debt in existing systems. This creates a critical juncture for shaping future identity architecture.
options: Promote W3C WebID as a foundation for decentralized identity ecosystems
Develop modular API layers for legacy system integration with new authentication methods
Establish a certification process for interoperable identity verification components
review: Standardization speed vs. system compatibility. Weakness: Options assume technical readiness without addressing legacy system constraints.

name: Stakeholder Incentive Structures
consequences: Aligning institutional incentives with platform neutrality could accelerate adoption but risks misalignment with short-term operational priorities. This requires careful calibration of rewards and penalties.
options: Create funding pools for public-sector agencies adopting resilient identity solutions
Implement performance metrics tied to platform dependency reduction targets
Develop a public recognition program for early-adopting organizations
review: Incentive alignment vs. operational disruption. Weakness: Options lack mechanisms to address immediate cost burdens on adopters.

name: Trust Model Reconfiguration
consequences: Shifts reliance from platform-specific trust signals to decentralized verification mechanisms. This risks complicating integration with existing MitID infrastructure while creating ambiguity in compliance with eIDAS2 requirements.
options: Redefine trust anchors to include open-source verification protocols
Establish decentralized identity verification through blockchain-based consensus
Integrate third-party audit mechanisms for platform neutrality
review: Balances proprietary trust models against open-standard alternatives. Weakness: Overlooks potential legal conflicts with existing eIDAS2 certification pathways.

name: Public Sector Procurement Alignment
consequences: Influences vendor behavior through contractual requirements. This could accelerate adoption of platform-neutral solutions but may provoke pushback from dominant platform providers.
options: Mandate platform-neutral authentication in all public-sector contracts
Incentivize vendors with compliance bonuses for open-standard adoption
Create a public procurement review board focused on digital sovereignty
review: Navigates procurement neutrality versus vendor concentration. Weakness: Underestimates the complexity of renegotiating existing platform contracts.

name: Stakeholder Coalition Dynamics
consequences: Builds collective pressure through diverse advocacy groups. This strengthens political momentum but risks diluting focus if coalition priorities diverge.
options: Form a cross-sector alliance with academic and industry experts
Engage civil society organizations to advocate for policy changes
Leverage international digital rights groups for external pressure
review: Coordinates fragmented interests against centralized platform power. Weakness: Fails to address potential conflicts between coalition members' strategic goals.

name: Technical Resilience Architecture
consequences: Develops fallback systems independent of platform APIs. This enhances continuity planning but may require significant reengineering of existing authentication workflows.
options: Design browser-based authentication layers decoupled from OS integrity checks
Implement hardware token integration with minimal platform dependencies
Create sandboxed environments for isolated authentication testing
review: Prioritizes system resilience over immediate deployment. Weakness: Risks creating parallel systems that remain disconnected from core infrastructure.

name: Regulatory Advocacy Pathways
consequences: Shapes policy through targeted engagement with oversight bodies. This can establish precedent for platform neutrality but requires sustained institutional buy-in.
options: Propose eIDAS2 amendments for certified fallback authentication requirements
Lobby for mandatory transparency reports on platform dependency risks
Develop model procurement language for EU-wide adoption
review: Aligns technical goals with regulatory frameworks. Weakness: Underestimates the time required to influence EU-level standard-setting processes.
